threats.title: Burns Accumulate address(0) in Holders Array Causing Permanent Distribution DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: Every burn operation (burn/burnFrom) triggers _beforeTokenTransfer with to=address(0). Since balanceOf(address(0)) is always 0, the check `this.balanceOf(to) != 0` is always false, causing `holders.push(address(0))` on every burn. The _afterTokenTransfer hook only attempts to remove `from` (the burner), never address(0). Over time, the holders array grows unboundedly with address(0) entries. distributeToAllHolders() processes each entry, eventually exceeding block gas limits. Even partial distribute() calls waste gas iterating over phantom entries.
threats.investigation_rationale: Burns are a public function via ERC20Burnable. Any token holder can burn 1 wei repeatedly to inflate the holders array. The address(0) entries are never cleaned up because _afterTokenTransfer only checks `from`, and no other function removes them.

proof_of_signal.pattern: During burn: to=address(0), balanceOf(address(0))=0 always, so holders.push(address(0)) executes on every burn

preconditions: Attacker holds any amount of LiquidInfrastructureERC20 tokens
burn() is not blocked (within MinDistributionPeriod, not locked)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: to=address(0) during burns always evaluates balanceOf(address(0))==0, triggering holders.push(address(0))
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No guard against pushing address(0); no cleanup mechanism for phantom entries
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing `if (to == address(0)) return;` check before exists check

analysis_questions: How many burns are needed to make distributeToAllHolders() exceed block gas limit?
Can partial distribute() work around the inflated array, or is gas cost per entry too high?
Is there any mechanism to remove address(0) entries from holders?
validation_requirements: Burn tokens N times and verify holders.length increases by N with address(0) entries
Calculate gas per holder entry in distribute() to determine DoS threshold
Verify distributeToAllHolders() reverts with OOG after sufficient burns

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker burns 1 wei per transaction thousands of times. Each burn pushes address(0) to holders. Once holders array contains enough entries (~10K+), distributeToAllHolders() exceeds block gas limit. Even partial distribute() calls spend significant gas on phantom entries. Protocol becomes unable to complete distributions efficiently, potentially permanently.
prerequisites: Token balance (even 1 wei sufficient for many burns)
burnAndDistribute or burn accessible

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent DoS on distribution mechanism; all accumulated revenue becomes inaccessible
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders cannot receive distributions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No on-chain recovery — no function to clean holders array

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for holders array state after burns

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: ~50K gas per holder entry in distribute(); block gas limit / 50K = max processable holders

threats.title: releaseManagedNFT Contains Dead Validation (require(true)) Allowing Silent NFT Release Failures
threats.hypothesis_description: releaseManagedNFT contains `require(true, 'unable to find released NFT in ManagedNFTs')` which always passes regardless of whether the NFT was actually found and removed from the ManagedNFTs array. If the provided nftContract address is not in ManagedNFTs (wrong address, already released, or never added via addManagedNFT), the NFT token is transferred out but ManagedNFTs is not modified. This leaves stale entries in ManagedNFTs or fails to clean up, causing subsequent withdrawFromManagedNFTs to revert when attempting to withdraw from an unowned NFT.
threats.investigation_rationale: The require(true) is demonstrably wrong code — it should be a boolean tracking whether the NFT was found and removed. This is a logic error that silently allows inconsistent state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(true, 'unable to find released NFT in ManagedNFTs') — literal true, never reverts

preconditions: Owner calls releaseManagedNFT with an address not present in ManagedNFTs array
This contract owns the NFT being released (transferFrom would succeed)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: require(true) where a boolean found flag should be used
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No actual validation that the NFT was found in and removed from ManagedNFTs
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use `bool found = false;` then `found = true;` inside the loop, then `require(found)`

analysis_questions: Can releaseManagedNFT be called with a duplicate NFT address?
What happens to withdrawFromManagedNFTs when ManagedNFTs contains a stale (unowned) entry?
Does the stale entry cause permanent withdrawal DoS?
validation_requirements: Call releaseManagedNFT with an NFT not in ManagedNFTs and verify it succeeds
Verify stale ManagedNFTs entries cause withdrawFromManagedNFTs to revert
Test withdrawal cycle completion with stale entries

Or if a different unmanaged NFT is released, ManagedNFTs retains stale entries while the untracked NFT is sent away. Stale entries block withdrawal cycles.

threats.title: _afterTokenTransfer Missing Break After Swap-and-Pop Skips Swapped Element
threats.hypothesis_description: In _afterTokenTransfer, when removing a holder via swap-and-pop (copying last element to position i, then pop), the loop does not break after removal. After swap-and-pop, `i` increments, causing the element that was swapped from the end into position i to be skipped entirely — it is never checked. This means: (1) if there are duplicate entries of the same address (from bug TECH-H001/H002), only the first is removed per call; (2) the swapped element may be incorrectly left in the array even if it should be checked for removal conditions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The missing break is a demonstrable code defect. Combined with the duplicate entry bugs, this creates a situation where phantom entries can never be fully cleaned up, compounding the DoS risk.

proof_of_signal.pattern: After swap-and-pop: holders[i] = holders[holders.length - 1]; holders.pop(); — no break statement; i++ on next iteration skips the swapped element

preconditions: An address with 0 post-transfer balance exists in holders
The address is not the last element in the array

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Swap-and-pop without break; loop continues and skips swapped element
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Element swapped from end to position i is at index i but loop advances to i+1

analysis_questions: Under what conditions does the skipped element matter?
Does this compound with the address(0) bug to prevent cleanup?
Can the swapped element be a different address that should remain, causing accidental removal?
validation_requirements: Create scenario where address appears multiple times, transfer all tokens away, verify cleanup removes only one entry
Verify that the last element swapped in is never checked in subsequent iterations

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Combined with TECH-H002: multiple address(0) entries exist. A mint to address(0)-adjacent entries triggers _afterTokenTransfer for from=address(0). Only the first address(0) occurrence is removed. The swapped element (which might also be address(0)) is skipped. Phantom entries persist and accumulate, compounding DoS risk.
prerequisites: Duplicate entries in holders array (from TECH-H001 or TECH-H002)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Persistent phantom entries cause gas waste and potential permanent distribution DoS
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders affected by degraded distribution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No recovery mechanism for corrupted holders array

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for swap-and-pop behavior with duplicate entries

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas overhead per phantom entry in distribution

threats.title: Integer Division Truncation in Entitlement Calculation Permanently Locks Low-Decimal Token Revenue
threats.hypothesis_description: In _beginDistribution, entitlement per unit is calculated as `balance / supply` using integer division. For tokens with fewer decimals than the ERC20's 18 decimals (e.g., USDC with 6 decimals), when balance < supply, entitlementPerUnit truncates to 0. For example, with 1000 LiquidInfrastructureERC20 tokens (1e21 wei supply) and 1000 USDC (1e9 units), entitlement = 1e9 / 1e21 = 0. All USDC revenue rounds to zero per unit and is permanently locked in the contract with no recovery mechanism. The documentation explicitly mentions USDC and USDT as target distributable tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The division-first formula (balance/supply then multiply by holder balance) is a well-known precision loss pattern. The specific concern is that with realistic supply and 6-decimal tokens, ALL revenue truncates to 0, not just dust. The project documentation confirms USDC/USDT are intended distributable tokens.
threats.config_evidence: Documentation states 'Vetted ERC20 stablecoins (e.g., USDC, DAI, USDT)' as external integrations. Deployment script uses test ERC20s.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 entitlement = balance / supply; — integer division truncates to 0 when balance < supply

preconditions: Distributable ERC20 has fewer decimals than 18 (e.g., USDC with 6 decimals)
Token balance in contract < totalSupply of LiquidInfrastructureERC20
This is the expected case for realistic USDC revenue vs 18-decimal supply

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Division before multiplication: balance / supply computed first, then multiplied by holder balance in distribute()
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: With 6-decimal USDC and 18-decimal supply, any USDC amount below 10^12 (i.e., $1M) truncates to 0 per unit
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should compute (balance * holderBalance) / supply per holder for better precision

analysis_questions: What is the realistic total supply range for this ERC20?
At what USDC balance does entitlement become non-zero?
Is accumulated dust recoverable by any mechanism?
validation_requirements: Calculate entitlement with realistic supply (e.g., 1000 tokens = 1e21 wei) and USDC balance (e.g., 1000 USDC = 1e9)
Verify entitlementPerUnit = 0 in this scenario
Verify funds remain permanently locked

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Protocol accumulates 10,000 USDC (1e10 units) from managed NFTs. With 1000 ERC20 tokens outstanding (1e21 supply), entitlementPerUnit = 1e10/1e21 = 0. Distribution distributes 0 to every holder. USDC remains in contract permanently. Next distribution accumulates more, same result. Revenue is permanently trapped.
prerequisites: Low-decimal distributable ERC20 (USDC/USDT)
ERC20 supply > distributable balance

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: 100% of low-decimal token revenue permanently locked in contract
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders receive zero distributions for these tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No recovery mechanism — no admin withdrawal for dust/trapped funds

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests with realistic decimal mismatch scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Immediate on first distribution with mismatched decimals
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing pre/post distribution balances

threats.title: setDistributableERC20s Callable During Active Distribution Causes Permanent Contract Lock
threats.hypothesis_description: setDistributableERC20s has no check for LockedForDistribution. If called during an active distribution, it changes the distributableERC20s array while erc20EntitlementPerUnit was sized for the old array. In distribute(), the inner loop iterates over `distributableERC20s.length` (new length) and accesses `erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j]` (old length). If the new array is longer, this causes an array out-of-bounds revert. Since LockedForDistribution is true and can only be cleared by completing the distribution (which now always reverts), the contract is permanently locked — no transfers, mints, or burns can ever occur again.
threats.investigation_rationale: The missing LockedForDistribution guard on setDistributableERC20s is a code defect. While it requires admin action, admins may use governance tools or automated scripts that don't coordinate with distribution state. The permanent lock impact makes this critical.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setDistributableERC20s has onlyOwner but no LockedForDistribution check; distribute() at lines 167-175 iterates distributableERC20s.length and indexes erc20EntitlementPerUnit

preconditions: Owner calls setDistributableERC20s while LockedForDistribution is true
New distributableERC20s array length differs from erc20EntitlementPerUnit length

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Array length mismatch: distributableERC20s.length (modified) != erc20EntitlementPerUnit.length (frozen at _beginDistribution)

analysis_questions: Can the admin accidentally trigger this via governance or script?
Is there any way to recover from the permanent lock?
What if the new array is shorter — does it silently distribute wrong tokens?
validation_requirements: Start distribution, call setDistributableERC20s with longer array, call distribute, verify OOB revert
Verify contract is permanently locked after this sequence
Test with shorter array to check silent misallocation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Distribution starts with 1 distributable ERC20 (erc20EntitlementPerUnit.length=1). Admin calls setDistributableERC20s with 3 tokens. Next distribute() call iterates j=0,1,2 but erc20EntitlementPerUnit only has index 0. Access to erc20EntitlementPerUnit[1] causes array OOB revert. Distribution can never complete. Contract permanently locked.
prerequisites: Admin calls setDistributableERC20s during active distribution

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All ERC20 tokens permanently frozen (no transfers, mints, burns); all reward tokens locked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders and the protocol itself

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for setDistributableERC20s during active distribution

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking LockedForDistribution state before admin calls

threats.title: nextWithdrawal Index Corruption When releaseManagedNFT Called During Partial Withdrawal Cycle
threats.hypothesis_description: releaseManagedNFT uses swap-and-pop on ManagedNFTs but does not adjust the nextWithdrawal index. If an NFT at index < nextWithdrawal is released during a partial withdrawal cycle, the element swapped from the end (which hasn't been withdrawn from yet) replaces the removed element and is skipped. Worse, if enough NFTs are released such that ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal, the completion check `nextWithdrawal == ManagedNFTs.length` never triggers (nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length), and nextWithdrawal is never reset to 0, permanently stalling the withdrawal cycle.
threats.investigation_rationale: The interaction between ManagedNFTs array modification and the withdrawal cursor is a multi-function state flow issue. The code does not guard against ManagedNFTs modification while nextWithdrawal > 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: releaseManagedNFT swap-and-pop does not adjust nextWithdrawal; withdrawFromManagedNFTs at lines 238-257 relies on nextWithdrawal == ManagedNFTs.length for completion

preconditions: Partial withdrawal in progress (nextWithdrawal > 0)
Owner calls releaseManagedNFT for an NFT at index < nextWithdrawal
Or enough NFTs released that ManagedNFTs.length < nextWithdrawal

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: ManagedNFTs mutation during active withdrawal cycle without adjusting nextWithdrawal
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length makes the reset condition unreachable

analysis_questions: Can nextWithdrawal exceed ManagedNFTs.length permanently?
Does this block all future withdrawal cycles?
Is there any way to reset nextWithdrawal without completing the cycle?
validation_requirements: Start withdrawal for 3 of 4 NFTs (nextWithdrawal=3), release 2 NFTs, verify nextWithdrawal > ManagedNFTs.length
Call withdrawFromManagedNFTs and verify cycle never completes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner starts withdrawing from 4 NFTs with numWithdrawals=2 (nextWithdrawal=2). Owner then releases NFTs at index 0 and 1 (swap-and-pop reduces array to 2). Now nextWithdrawal=2 == ManagedNFTs.length=2, which actually completes the cycle correctly. But if owner releases NFT at index 3 (last) while nextWithdrawal=2, array becomes [0,1,2], length=3, and cycle continues normally. The dangerous case: release at index < nextWithdrawal before cycle completes, making length < nextWithdrawal.
prerequisites: Owner action during partial withdrawal
Specific timing of release vs withdrawal state

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders lose access to future distributions

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for ManagedNFTs modification during active withdrawal cycle

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring nextWithdrawal vs ManagedNFTs.length

threats.title: Disapproved Holders Dilute Distribution Entitlements for Approved Holders
threats.hypothesis_description: The entitlement calculation in _beginDistribution uses totalSupply() as the denominator, which includes tokens held by disapproved holders. During distribution, disapproved holders are skipped (isApprovedHolder check), but their proportional share is not redistributed. It remains in the contract and is included in the NEXT distribution's balance — but totalSupply still includes their tokens, so the dilution persists indefinitely. Approved holders are systematically underpaid relative to their actual share of the approved supply.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetry between entitlement calculation (uses totalSupply including disapproved) and actual distribution (skips disapproved) creates a persistent fund leak. Disapproved holders' tokens cannot be forcibly burned, so the dilution is permanent until the holder is re-approved or voluntarily burns.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 supply = this.totalSupply(); entitlement = balance / supply; — totalSupply includes disapproved holders' tokens

preconditions: At least one holder with non-zero balance is disapproved
Distribution occurs while disapproved holder's balance inflates totalSupply

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: totalSupply used as denominator but disapproved holders' balances skipped in distribution
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Disapproved holder owning 50% of supply reduces approved holders' per-unit entitlement by 50%
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use sum of approved holder balances as denominator, or force-burn disapproved tokens

analysis_questions: What fraction of supply could realistically be disapproved?
Can a disapproved holder ever recover their tokens?
Does the undistributed portion accumulate and eventually become distributable if the holder is re-approved?

Verify undistributed portion stays in contract and compounds over distributions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Holder A owns 50% of supply and gets disapproved. Every distribution calculates entitlementPerUnit = balance / totalSupply (includes A's 50%). During distribution, A is skipped, so 50% of entitlements go uncollected. Approved holders receive half their expected share per cycle. Over N distributions, a significant fraction of revenue is trapped.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All approved holders receive reduced distributions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Re-approving the holder would allow them to receive future distributions, but past cycles are lost

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Persistent across all distributions while disapproved
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing totalSupply vs sum of approved holder balances

threats.title: addManagedNFT Allows Duplicate NFT Entries Creating Double-Withdrawal and Release Issues
threats.hypothesis_description: addManagedNFT only checks that the contract owns the NFT but does not check whether the NFT is already in the ManagedNFTs array. If the owner calls addManagedNFT(nft) twice, the same NFT address appears twice in ManagedNFTs. During withdrawFromManagedNFTs, the first withdrawal drains the NFT's balance; the second withdrawal for the same NFT gets 0 (wasting gas). When releaseManagedNFT is called, only one occurrence is removed (the loop breaks), leaving a stale duplicate entry. Subsequent withdrawal cycles hit the stale entry (NFT no longer owned), causing reverts.
threats.investigation_rationale: Combined with the require(true) bug in releaseManagedNFT, this creates a compounding state corruption. The stale entry from an incomplete duplicate removal cannot be detected or removed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ManagedNFTs.push(nftContract) without checking for existing entry; no deduplication

preconditions: Owner calls addManagedNFT with same NFT address more than once
Contract owns the NFT at time of each call

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No duplicate check before push to ManagedNFTs
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: releaseManagedNFT with break only removes first occurrence, leaving stale duplicate
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Mapping to track managed NFT membership, or duplicate check before push

analysis_questions: Can addManagedNFT be called for the same NFT twice?
Does releaseManagedNFT handle duplicates correctly?
What is the impact of a stale ManagedNFTs entry on withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Call addManagedNFT(nft) twice, verify ManagedNFTs contains duplicate
Release the NFT, verify one entry remains

threats.title: Disapproved Holders' Tokens Are Permanently Trapped with No Exit Path
threats.hypothesis_description: When a holder is disapproved via disapproveHolder(), they can no longer transfer their tokens (allowlist check in _beforeTokenTransfer reverts for non-zero `to`). Their only exit path is burning, but burn() calls _beforeMintOrBurn() which requires a distribution to have occurred within MinDistributionPeriod. If _isPastMinDistributionPeriod returns true, the holder must first trigger a distribution before burning. However, burnAndDistribute() calls distributeToAllHolders() which may exceed gas limits if the holders array is bloated. The disapproved holder's tokens and their distribution entitlement are effectively trapped.
threats.investigation_rationale: This represents a design flaw where disapproval creates a state with no viable exit path for the affected holder. The combination of allowlist enforcement on transfers and distribution requirement on burns creates a deadlock for disapproved holders.

proof_of_signal.pattern: isApprovedHolder(to) check on transfers; combined with _beforeMintOrBurn distribution requirement on burns at lines 137-142

preconditions: Holder is disapproved while still holding tokens
MinDistributionPeriod has elapsed (making burns require prior distribution)
Distribution is difficult to complete (bloated holders array or other DoS)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Disapproved holder cannot transfer (allowlist check) and may not be able to burn (distribution requirement)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No mechanism for the holder or admin to recover trapped tokens
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Allow burns without distribution requirement for disapproved holders, or add admin force-burn

analysis_questions: Can a disapproved holder call burnAndDistribute successfully?
What happens if distributeToAllHolders exceeds gas limit?
Can the owner help by calling distribute() in batches before the holder burns?
validation_requirements: Disapprove holder with balance, attempt transfer (verify revert), attempt burn during stale period (verify revert)
Test burnAndDistribute path under various conditions

threats.title: Permanent distribution lock if a single recipient/token transfer reverts (blacklist/pause/nonstandard ERC20), blocking all transfers/mints/burns
threats.hypothesis_description: distribute() sets LockedForDistribution=true in _beginDistribution(), then performs external ERC20 `transfer()` calls inside nested loops. If any transfer reverts (e.g., token is paused/blacklists a holder; token is nonstandard and reverts on ABI decoding; insufficient balance due to rebasing), the entire distribute() call reverts and the distribution remains locked (LockedForDistribution stays true because state changes prior to revert are rolled back to pre-call, but the lock can still be re-entered and get stuck across attempts if every attempt fails at the same holder/token pair). Practically, if one holder cannot receive a given distributable token, finishing a distribution may be impossible, freezing all ERC20 transfers due to the lock requirement in _beforeTokenTransfer().
threats.investigation_rationale: Real-world stablecoins (USDT/USDC) can blacklist addresses; many tokens can be paused. Any single problematic recipient or misconfigured distributable token can brick distributions and, by design, block all transfers/mints/burns until distribution completes.
deployment_assumptions: At least one distributableERC20 either (a) reverts on transfer for some recipients (blacklist/pause) or (b) is nonstandard (no boolean return) causing revert
At least one such recipient exists in holders[] and is approved at time of transfer attempt
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation states distributable tokens include vetted stablecoins (e.g., USDC, DAI, USDT) but no on-chain enforcement of token compliance; distribute() uses raw IERC20.transfer() without SafeERC20.

proof_of_signal.pattern: distribute(): nested loop calls `IERC20(distributableERC20s[j]).transfer(recipient, entitlement)` with no SafeERC20 wrapper; distribution lock blocks all transfers via _beforeTokenTransfer()

preconditions: LockedForDistribution can be entered (MinDistributionPeriod elapsed, supply>0, holders>0)
At least one (recipient, token) pair causes `transfer()` to revert consistently
No admin emergency-unlock function exists (none in code)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external token transfers inside distribution loop without per-recipient error handling; hard global lock on token movements while distribution in progress
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: one bad recipient/token can prevent completion forever, freezing ERC20 operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No try/catch around transfer, no skip-on-revert strategy, no admin forced unlock

analysis_questions: Is LockedForDistribution ever set true and persisted across calls, or always reverted on failing transfers (confirm state rollback behavior and whether partial progress can be resumed)?
Can distribution be resumed after revert, or does it always fail at the same index because nextDistributionRecipient doesn't advance?
What happens if a token returns false vs reverts vs no return value under Solidity 0.8.12 ABI expectations?
validation_requirements: Simulate a distributable token that reverts for one recipient; ensure distribute() cannot progress past that recipient
Test with a no-return ERC20 mock (USDT-like) to see if it reverts and bricks distributions
Confirm that mint/burn/transfer are blocked while LockedForDistribution is true and that there is no recovery path

Anyone calling distribute() will always revert at the attacker’s index, making it impossible to complete distributions. Because transfers/mints/burns are blocked during distribution, the ERC20 becomes effectively frozen whenever a distribution attempt is made and cannot be completed.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All ERC20 holders and protocol operators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational only (change distributableERC20s, remove holder, or migrate), but may be blocked by lock/logic depending on exact failure mode

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable repeated reverts at same distribution index; LockedForDistribution toggling behavior to verify

threats.title: Anyone can grief withdrawals by manipulating nextWithdrawal progress with a reverting/hostile ManagedNFT, stalling revenue collection
threats.hypothesis_description: withdrawFromManagedNFTs() is public and iterates ManagedNFTs calling external `getThresholds()` and `withdrawBalancesTo()`. If any ManagedNFT in the list reverts (e.g., not actually owned/approved, malicious contract at that address, or legitimate NFT with misconfigured thresholds causing downstream token transfer revert), the whole withdrawal batch reverts and nextWithdrawal does not advance. threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidInfrastructureNFT inherits both `ERC721` and `OwnableApprovableERC721`, and the latter also inherits `ERC721`. threats.title: Duplicate Holder Array Entries via Zero-Value Transfers Enable Reward Theft and Permanent Distribution DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: Any address (even unapproved, with zero balance) can call transfer(approvedAddress, 0) repeatedly. In _beforeTokenTransfer, balanceOf(to) is checked BEFORE the transfer executes. Since the target has 0 balance and receives 0 tokens, balanceOf(to)==0 on every call, causing holders.push(to) on every call. This creates unbounded duplicate entries in the holders array. During distribution, each duplicate entry triggers a separate entitlement calculation (erc20EntitlementPerUnit * balanceOf(address)), causing the same address to receive N times their fair share. When total distributions exceed the contract's token balance, the ERC20 transfer reverts, permanently locking the contract in distribution state (LockedForDistribution=true), blocking all transfers, mints, and burns forever.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a compound code defect: (1) _beforeTokenTransfer checks balance pre-transfer allowing repeated 0-value push, (2) no duplicate check on holders.push, (3) no minimum transfer amount. The distribution loop iterates over all entries including duplicates, computing full entitlement for each. Any external address can trigger this without tokens or approval.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bool exists = (this.balanceOf(to) != 0); if (!exists) { holders.push(to); } — checked pre-transfer, no dedup, allows 0-amount transfers to push duplicates

preconditions: At least one approved holder address exists with zero balance
LockedForDistribution is false
No minimum transfer amount enforced (confirmed: OZ ERC20 allows 0-value transfer)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: entitlement = erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] * this.balanceOf(recipient) computed per holders array entry including duplicates
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call transfer(approvedAddr, 0) N times → N entries for same address → N * entitlement per distribution
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If target address has balance B and appears N times, they receive N * (balance/supply) * B instead of (balance/supply) * B. Net theft = (N-1) * fair_share.

Test that unapproved addresses can call transfer(approvedAddr, 0) successfully

threats.title: MinDistributionPeriod=0 Creates Permanent Mint/Burn Deadlock
threats.hypothesis_description: If MinDistributionPeriod is set to 0, _isPastMinDistributionPeriod() returns true immediately after any distribution ends (same block), because (block.number - LastDistribution) = 0 >= 0. This causes _beforeMintOrBurn() to revert with 'must distribute before minting or burning'. But distribution was just completed, and calling distribute() again immediately completes (no holders to process), resetting LastDistribution to the current block. The mint/burn check immediately fails again. This creates an infinite deadlock where minting and burning are permanently blocked.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script sets MinDistributionPeriod to 10, but this parameter has no setter, so it's only set at construction. If deployed with 0, the protocol is permanently broken. The code defect is that _isPastMinDistributionPeriod returns true when period=0, making the 'distribute first' requirement unsatisfiable in the same block.
deployment_assumptions: MinDistributionPeriod is set to 0 at construction

proof_of_signal.pattern: return (block.number - LastDistribution) >= MinDistributionPeriod; — when MinDistributionPeriod=0, this is always true after first distribution, making mint/burn permanently blocked

At least one distribution has occurred

threats.title: Silent Transfer Failure in distribute() Permanently Steals Holder Entitlements
threats.hypothesis_description: In distribute(), the ERC20 transfer to recipients uses `if (toDistribute.transfer(recipient, entitlement))` — a conditional that handles false returns by simply skipping the payout. If a distributable ERC20 returns false instead of reverting (e.g., token with transfer restrictions, blacklists, or non-standard behavior), the recipient's entitlement for that distribution cycle is silently lost. The tokens remain in the contract and become part of the next distribution's pool, but the affected holder permanently loses their share for that cycle. No event indicates the failure.
threats.investigation_rationale: Some ERC20 tokens return false on failed transfers rather than reverting (e.g., some implementations with blacklists or pause mechanisms). The if-check means the contract treats failure as acceptable, but the holder has no recourse to claim their skipped entitlement. The emitted Distribution event shows receipts[j]=0 for the failed transfer, but this is indistinguishable from a legitimate zero entitlement.
deployment_assumptions: At least one distributable ERC20 returns false on transfer failure rather than reverting
threats.config_evidence: No SafeERC20 used. Project targets stablecoins (USDC, USDT) — some implementations on some chains may return false for blacklisted addresses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (toDistribute.transfer(recipient, entitlement)) { receipts[j] = entitlement; } — silent skip on false return

Affected recipient is an approved holder with non-zero balance

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No attacker profit — holder loss. Skipped tokens roll into next distribution pool benefiting other holders

threats.title: setDistributableERC20s Callable During Active Distribution Causes Permanent Contract Lock
threats.hypothesis_description: setDistributableERC20s has no check for LockedForDistribution. If the owner calls it during an active distribution (LockedForDistribution=true), the distributableERC20s array changes while erc20EntitlementPerUnit retains its old length. If the new array is LONGER than the old one, the inner loop in distribute() (`for j = 0; j < distributableERC20s.length; j++`) will access erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] out of bounds, causing a permanent revert. Since LockedForDistribution is already true, the contract is permanently frozen — no transfers, mints, or burns possible.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a missing state guard: setDistributableERC20s should check !LockedForDistribution. The code path is: _beginDistribution sets erc20EntitlementPerUnit (length N), then admin calls setDistributableERC20s (length M > N), then distribute() tries erc20EntitlementPerUnit[N..M-1] which doesn't exist → revert. No recovery path exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setDistributableERC20s has onlyOwner but no LockedForDistribution check, while distribute() iterates distributableERC20s.length indexing into erc20EntitlementPerUnit

preconditions: Owner calls setDistributableERC20s while LockedForDistribution=true

threats.title: Disapproved Holders Dilute Approved Holders' Revenue Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: Entitlement per unit is calculated as `balance / totalSupply()` where totalSupply includes balances of disapproved holders. During distribution, disapproved holders are skipped (isApprovedHolder check), but their proportional share is NOT redistributed — it remains in the contract. Disapproved holders cannot transfer tokens (allowlist blocks receivers), only burn. This means: (1) approved holders receive less than their fair share relative to active supply, (2) the skipped portion accumulates as inaccessible dust, (3) disapproved holders' tokens still dilute the denominator indefinitely.

A disapproved holder with 50% of supply causes all approved holders to receive only ~50% of what they're entitled to. The remaining 50% sits permanently in the contract with no recovery mechanism.

threats.title: releaseManagedNFT require(true) Bypasses Validation Enabling Stale ManagedNFTs Array
threats.hypothesis_description: releaseManagedNFT contains `require(true, 'unable to find released NFT in ManagedNFTs')` which ALWAYS passes regardless of whether the NFT was found in the array. threats.title: ManagedNFTs Array Modification During Partial Withdrawal Corrupts nextWithdrawal Index
threats.hypothesis_description: withdrawFromManagedNFTs uses a stateful cursor (nextWithdrawal) to track progress through partial withdrawal batches. If nextWithdrawal exceeds the new array length, the loop in withdrawFromManagedNFTs starts at an index beyond the array, processes nothing, and cannot reset.

threats.title: setDistributableERC20s Callable During Active Distribution Causes Permanent Lock
threats.hypothesis_description: setDistributableERC20s has no check for LockedForDistribution. If the owner calls it during an active distribution, the distributableERC20s array changes while erc20EntitlementPerUnit was sized for the old array. If the new array is shorter, accessing distributableERC20s[j] and erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] in distribute() will have mismatched lengths. If the new array is longer, erc20EntitlementPerUnit[j] will revert with index out of bounds. Either way, the distribution reverts permanently, and LockedForDistribution stays true, freezing all contract operations.

Is there an ops control preventing add/release during ongoing withdrawal cycles?
Is there monitoring that detects a stuck nextWithdrawal cursor?
validation_requirements: Test: call withdrawFromManagedNFTs(1) to set nextWithdrawal>0, then addManagedNFT/release, then attempt to finish cycle; observe cursor behavior
Check for conditions where nextWithdrawal can exceed ManagedNFTs.length and never reset

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Start batched withdrawals so nextWithdrawal is non-zero.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Observe skipped withdrawals or failure to reset nextWithdrawal, blocking future cycles.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Revenue not collected; distributions may underpay or fail due to missing funds; operational confusion
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium (may require manual remediation via custom transactions or redeploy)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: WithdrawalStarted emitted but no WithdrawalFinished for long periods
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Some ManagedNFTs never emit Withdrawal events over multiple cycles
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Portfolio management txs interleaved with withdrawal batches
